mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 2 Series M240i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M240i (B58) engine typically costs £5,200–£8,500 supply only depending on generation. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 2 Series M240i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 straight-six is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. It has proven robust in everything from the M240i to the Toyota Supra. However, it has known weak points: PCV valve failure (especially on tuned cars), coolant leaks, valve cover gasket degradation, and VANOS solenoid wear. Regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M240i?

acceptedAnswer.text: PCV valve failure (rough idle, misfires, oil in intake), coolant leaks (low coolant warnings, potential overheating), valve cover gasket failure (oil leaks from the top of the engine), oil filter housing issues, and VANOS solenoid wear. All are manageable with preventative maintenance and early detection.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an M240i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well-maintained M240i (values £18,000–£40,000+ depending on year and mileage), a £6,000–£7,500 reconditioned engine is 15–35% of the vehicle's value — often worth it for a solid example. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M240i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code (B58B30 or B58B30M2) is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The F22/F23 generation (2017–2021) uses the B58B30. The G42 generation (2022–present) uses the updated B58B30M2. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the PCV valve, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: PCV valve failure is always repairable without replacing the engine. A PCV diaphragm repair kit costs £100–£300 and restores proper operation. However, if the PCV failure has led to oil contamination or other issues, more extensive work may be required. At the first sign of rough idle or misfires, have the PCV system inspected.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW 2 Series M240i Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Most owners — best cost/warranty balance for the B58

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current M240i B58 era. Older F22-generation cars may command slightly lower pricing — see the Era Map above for generation-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an M240i engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). Some specialists quote approximately 15 hours of labour for B58 replacement. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

problems.title: PCV Valve Failure (B58)
problems.symptoms: Misfires, rough idle, oil in the intake or charge pipe, white smoke from exhaust, check engine light.
problems.typicalMileage: 50,000–80,000 miles (can be earlier on tuned cars)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can cause drivability issues

problems.recommendation: Though the B58 engine has proven mostly reliable, it has one well-known problem: failure of the PCV valve. This issue is especially common on cars with upgraded turbos. The PCV diaphragm cap is prone to failure with age and mileage, as the rubber portion naturally dries and cracks over time. Signs include misfires, rough idle, and oil in the intake. The good news is that this is easily fixed with an affordable repair kit.

problems.title: Coolant Leaks (B58)
problems.symptoms: Low coolant warning, coolant loss with no visible external leak, sweet smell from engine bay, overheating.
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,200 (diagnosis and repair — coolant reservoir, water pump, or charge cooler)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant loss is detected

problems.recommendation: The B58 engine can be prone to coolant leaks. Problems with the coolant reservoir cap seals can bleed coolant slowly, whereas if the leak is heavy, check the charge-cooler hasn't been punctured. Some owners have reported the need to top up coolant on a monthly basis. If you notice a "Coolant Low" message, investigate further immediately. Regular coolant system inspections are recommended.

problems.title: Valve Cover Gasket Failure (B58)
problems.symptoms: Oil leak from the top of the engine, burning oil smell, visible oil seepage around the valve cover.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: Valve cover gasket failure is one of the most common issues with the BMW B58 engine. The gasket is made of rubber and has a tendency to become brittle, crack and start to leak due to constant exposure to heat and engine oil. Obvious oil leaks are the most common symptom. Leaking oil can saturate and degrade vacuum, coolant, and air intake hoses, and can foul spark plugs. Regular inspections can catch this before it becomes a major issue.

problems.title: Oil Filter Housing Issues
problems.symptoms: Oil leaks around the oil filter housing area, oil warning light, low oil pressure.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can lead to oil starvation

problems.recommendation: A common problem with the B58 is that upon servicing, the oil filter has a chance of sticking and not coming out cleanly. This results in longer service times. Many owners of older B58s have experienced a leaky oil filter housing, with the average lifespan for the component appearing to be around eight years. Regular oil changes (every 12,000 miles, or 6–8,000 miles if driven hard) are recommended.

problems.title: VANOS Solenoid Wear
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, hesitation on acceleration, reduced power, check engine light.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can affect variable valve timing

problems.recommendation: Common B58 engine problems include VANOS solenoid failure due to O-ring wear, which can affect variable valve timing. The timing chain and tensioners are inaccessible at the back of the engine, making timing-related repairs an engine-out job. If you experience rough idle or hesitation, have the VANOS system inspected promptly.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW M240i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Affordable repair; catch it before drivability issues worsen
Diagnosis and repair is straightforward; catch it before overheating
Standard maintenance; replace before oil damages other components
The B58 is robust; catastrophic failures are extremely rare

buyingChecks.title: Buying This BMW M240i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with oil changes at 8,000–12,000 mile intervals
* No coolant loss — check the "Coolant Low" message on the iDrive
* No cold-start rattles — the B58's timing chain is at the rear of the engine
* PCV system functioning correctly (no rough idle, no oil in intake)
* ZF 8-speed automatic transmission serviced every 60,000–80,000 miles
avoidIf: * No proof of regular oil changes — the B58 is a performance engine that demands maintenance
* Coolant loss with no diagnosis — cooling system repairs can be complex
* Rough idle or misfires with no diagnosis — PCV or VANOS issues likely
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — the B58 can hide expensive faults
* Excessive oil consumption — indicates potential piston ring or valve seal issues

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW M240i represents a golden era for BMW's compact performance cars — the B58 straight-six delivers effortless power, a glorious soundtrack, and proven reliability. It's a significant step up from the N55 it replaced. The B58 has proven mostly reliable, but it's not without its quirks: PCV valve failure, coolant leaks, and valve cover gasket degradation are the key watchpoints. Buy on service history, maintain on a shortened schedule, and this is one of the most rewarding modern BMWs you can own.

cta.label: → Compare BMW M240i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 2 Series M240i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattles (timing chain is at the rear — engine-out job if it fails)
* Service History — Oil changes at 8,000–12,000 mile intervals; 6–8,000 if driven hard
* Coolant Level — Check for "Coolant Low" messages; B58s can be prone to coolant leaks
* Oil Level — Monitor via iDrive (no dipstick); excessive consumption indicates issues
* Idle Quality — Any roughness or misfires? PCV or VANOS issues may be present
* Smoke Check — White smoke = PCV or coolant issues; blue smoke = oil burning
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? PCV, VANOS, or cooling codes are most common
* Transmission Service — ZF 8-speed should be serviced every 60,000–80,000 miles
